title: Today's AI Brief: Can AI tell if your script will make a hit film?
summary: AI's role in predicting film success and concerns over AI-aided bioweapons
imageAlt: Image from theverge.com for Can AI tell if your script will make a hit film?
imageCaption: Can AI tell if your script will make a hit film? (theverge.com)

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your AI Brief. Today, we're looking at a couple of interesting developments in the world of artificial intelligence. First up, can AI predict if your script will make a hit film? According to a report on The Verge, a company called Quilty claims it can use AI to predict box office success. This is an intriguing development, as it suggests that AI can analyze scripts and determine their potential for success.

I'm not sure if I'd trust AI to make Hollywood decisions just yet, but it's an interesting idea. I mean, can you imagine if AI had predicted the success of some of the classics? Maybe we'd have a lot more sequels to 'The Shawshank Redemption' out there. On a more serious note, it's also worth considering what makes a film successful - is it just the script, or are there other factors at play?

AI leaders are also calling for tougher protections against AI-aided bioweapons. This is a concern that's been raised by industry rivals, who are worried that their technology could be used to develop biological weapons. It's a sobering reminder of the potential risks associated with AI, and it's clear that this is an area where the industry needs to be careful.

Can AI tell if your script will make a hit film?, from theverge.com, is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: AI Entertainment Film Can AI tell if your script will make a hit film? Quilty claims it can predict box office success. by Charles Pulliam-Moore Jun 5, 2026, 1:57 PM UTC The Verge, Getty Images Charles Pulliam-Moore is a reporter focusing on film, TV, and pop culture. Before The Verge, he wrote about comic books, labor, race, and more at io9 and Gizmodo for... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

AI leaders call for tougher protections against AI-aided bioweapons, from theverge.com, is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: AI News Science AI leaders call for tougher protections against AI-aided bioweapons AI industry rivals rarely agree, but they’re all concerned their technology could be used to develop biological weapons. AI industry rivals rarely agree, but they’re all concerned their technology could be used to develop biological weapons. by Robert Hart Jun 4, 2026, 12:12... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
